points.text: Examine gateway logs for 502/504 patterns and timestamps to correlate with backend latency spikes.
Enable tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to measure latency of each downstream HTTP call and DB query.
Compare post‑release metrics with baseline: request rate, DB CPU, connection pool usage, thread counts.
Identify slow or failing downstream services; add timeout and circuit‑breaker settings to prevent hangs.
Profile database: look for long‑running queries, missing indexes, lock contention; consider query optimization or read replicas.
Verify backend thread/worker pool sizes and queue lengths; increase if saturation is observed.
Temporarily roll back the release or disable new features to see if errors disappear, confirming code change impact.
Implement rate limiting or back‑pressure at the gateway to avoid overwhelming downstream services during spikes.
Enable the DB slow‑query log and capture statements that exceed a low latency threshold.
Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the high‑CPU queries to spot full table scans or missing indexes.
Inspect lock contention (e.g., pg_locks or equivalent) to see if blocking is inflating CPU usage.
Check the application connection‑pool size; excessive concurrent connections can saturate the DB CPU.
Add appropriate covering indexes or rewrite queries to limit rows processed and reduce CPU load.
Collect gateway error logs with timestamps to correlate 502/504 spikes with backend request latency.
Enable detailed request tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) across downstream services to identify slow calls.
Monitor backend CPU, memory, and thread pool usage; high DB CPU may indicate query contention.
Inspect database query plans and lock statistics; add indexes or rewrite queries to reduce CPU load.
Check connection pool limits on the backend; exhaustion can cause timeouts seen as 502/504.
Verify load balancer and gateway timeout settings; increase them if backend processing exceeds defaults.
Review Linux kernel limits (net.core.somaxconn, file descriptors) and raise them if hitting caps.
Implement circuit breaker or retry logic for downstream calls to prevent cascading failures.
Scale the database vertically or add read replicas to distribute load and lower CPU pressure.
Add caching for frequent read queries to reduce DB hits and improve overall response time.
